rationale: This is the classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, showing the formation of a stable tert-butyl carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid (sulfuric acid) catalysis. This carbocation is then trapped by acetonitrile, which, after hydrolysis, yields N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and products are simple, acyclic, and lack complex functional groups, making it a clear and unambiguous representation of the core transformation.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic compounds (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with reactions involving aromatic rings, complex ring systems, or other reactive functional groups. The use of a tertiary alcohol as the carbocation precursor is highly characteristic of the Ritter reaction and less common in other named reactions, thus minimizing potential collisions.
